old_title: Andy Burnham latest: PM cuts VAT on electricity bills in first policy announcement
new_title: Trump furious at Miliband's appointment as foreign secretary
old_text:
Andy Burnham has announced a fresh tax cut to remove VAT from domestic electricity bills from 1 October, the first in what is expected to be a series of policy announcements on Tuesday morning.
Chancellor John Healey said the cut would be funded from cancelling the Digital ID programme. "For too long, too many people have struggled with the cost of living," he said. "Today's energy tax cut will give families some breathing room on bills, and provide some reassurance this winter."
Mr Burnham was still appointing his cabinet late into Monday night, with Angela Rayner returning to government as housing secretary, Healey named as chancellor and Wes Streeting appointed as defence secretary.
Ed Miliband secured a place as foreign secretary and Shabana Mahmood is remaining as home secretary.
Louise Haigh, who managed Mr Burnham's Makerfield campaign, has been rewarded for her loyalty with a job at the heart of government as first secretary of state and chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster.
Chancellor Rachel Reeves, deputy prime minister David Lammy and housing secretary Steve Reed, all allies of Sir Keir Starmer, were sacked by the former mayor of Greater Manchester and have returned to the backbenches.
Shadow defence secretary says new policies have 'got to last more than a few minutes' of scrutiny
Shadow defence secretary James Cartlidge has said new policies have "got to last more than a few minutes" of scrutiny, during a discussion on the Prime Minister's announcement that new VAT cuts will be partly funded by scrapping Sir Keir Starmer's digital ID project.
Mr Cartlidge's comments came after former chief secretary to the prime minister Darren Jones posted on X that it is "good news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills" but "the DigitalID program was unfunded".
The shadow defence secretary told Times Radio that the OBR (Office for Budget Responsibility) also said last year that the digital ID card scheme has not been funded.
He said: "They (the OBR) literally said that, so you can't fund a tax cut with... with savings from a programme that itself wasn't funded.
"So you know, big question here, backed up by someone who - as you can rightly say - was only recently was in the heart of government under Labour - so it's a fairly extraordinary position... I certainly agree with the new prime minister that cost of living is a big issue, I think we all agree with that, it's still the number one thing that comes up on the doorstep with my constituents.
"But if you're going to have policies to deal with it, they've got to last more than a few minutes in terms of scrutiny, to be honest."
Sacked Cabinet minister disputes funding claims for Burnham's first major policy
The Independent's political reporter Athena Stavrou reports:
A Starmer loyalist who was sacked from a senior Cabinet role by Andy Burnham has disputed the new prime minister's funding claims underpinning his first major policy.
The Burnham government have said a fresh tax cut to remove VAT from domestic electricity bills would be funded from cancelling the Digital ID programme.
But shortly after the policy was announced, former Chief Secretary to the prime minister Darren Jones publicly claimed the policy was not funded.
He wrote on X: "Good news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills. It's a simple way for families to save a few quid, and to mechanically help to keep inflation that little bit lower.
"But the DigitalID program was unfunded. The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget."
He now becomes the first Labour backbencher to publicly criticise the Burnham government, something the former mayor may have to get used to.
US politics are 'robust' and Miliband's appointment won't be an issue, minister says
The Independent's political reporter Athena Stavrou reports:
A government minister has insisted Ed Miliband's appointment as foreign secretary won't negatively impact Britain's relationship with the US, despite having vocally criticised Donald Trump in the past.
Mr Miliband, who will have to work closely with the US administration, previously called the US president a "racist, misogynistic, self-confessed groper".
But asked if this could be an issue in his new role, business secretary Jonathan Reynolds told Sky News: "No, I think as ever that this government will work with allies across the world.
"I know from my experience last time on some of the trade deal negotiations, it can be a robust exchange with our US friends and allies. But they will stand up for what they believe their national interest is, and they will do the same."
He added: "Well, sometimes I reflect on things that members of the US administration have themselves said about different people in US politics. So they are quite a robust bunch over there in the US and I know they'll have their priorities. We've got ours.
"Can we work together on lots of things in mutual interest in both countries? Absolutely, we can."
Cabinet minister hails 'tremendous' VAT cut on energy bills
The Independent's political reporter Athena Stavrou reports:
One of Andy Burnham's newly appointed government minister's has hailed his decision to cut Vat on energy bills as "a tremendous announcement for the first day of the new cabinet".
Business secretary Jonathan Reynolds said the policy is a "clear statement of intent" from the Burnham government on how it will deal with the cost of living crisis.
"It's about the government's priorities," he told Sky News. "It's about showing we understand people need some breathing space for pressure that they are under, and being able to make an announcement like this, I think, is a real statement."
Burnham's new business secretary 'glad to be back'
The Independent's political reporter Athena Stavrou reports:
Andy Burnham's newly appointed business secretary Jonathan Reynolds is the first minister to be up on the morning broadcast round.
Mr Reynolds, who first served as Starmer's business secretary before being moved to the role as chief whip, said he was "very glad to be back" in the role.
Asked if he did not enjoy the role of chief whip, he said: "It's an important job. It's a privilege to serve in any capacity."
But look, I think for the kind of things that I think make the biggest difference to the country - I still get stopped in the street by people who work for British Steel, or what we did with Harland and Wolff, or the trade deal.
"So I think the chance to serve in that it's a privilege in any capacity. But this is what I love doing."
Questions raised over how cutting the digital ID project funds VAT cut
Former chief secretary to the prime minister Darren Jones said the digital ID project was unfunded and questioned how the VAT cut would be paid for.
In a post on X he said: "Good news that VAT will be cut on electricity bills.
"It's a simple way for families to save a few quid, and to mechanically help to keep inflation that little bit lower.
"But the DigitalID program was unfunded. The government will have to set out how it will pay for its new policies at the budget."
Government determined to 'creating real opportunities for young people'
The government aims to reform education to help young people get into work.
"The reality is too many young people are still locked out of work," a Government spokesperson said.
"For too long, governments have paid for failure rather than invested in people's success.
"We're determined to turn that around by creating real opportunities for young people, reforming education so everyone has a clear path to a good job and providing the support people need to stay and get on in work."
Watch: Angela Rayner returns in cabinet reshuffle as Andy Burnham becomes prime minister
The 'economy is stuck in the slow lane' warns MP
Responding to unemployment and borrowing statistics published by the ONS today, which show unemployment has risen and public borrowing at £16bn, Liberal Democrat Treasury Spokesperson Daisy Cooper MP said: "The new Chancellor needs to turn things around, and fast. The economy is stuck in the slow lane while job seekers and families pay the price.
"Healey now needs to urgently tackle unemployment, by scrapping Rachel Reeves' unfair jobs tax that is stifling businesses and slamming the door shut on job opportunities.
"Today's borrowing figures also show the huge pressure on our public finances. The only real answer is to boost growth, and protect our economy from the global shocks it faces, like Trump's illegal Iran war. That's why Andy Burnham must immediately begin negotiations for a new growth defence partnership with the EU, including joining the Single Market."
Government borrowing fell to £16 billion last month
UK Government borrowing fell to £16 billion last month, below what forecasters were expecting thanks to lower inflation-linked debt interest costs, according to official figures.
It means June's public sector net borrowing figure was £7.9 billion, or 33.1%, less than the same month in 2025, the Office for National Statistics (ONS) said.
It was also £300 million below the Office for Budget Responsibility's (OBR) forecast, and £2 billion less than what most economists had been expecting.
Borrowing between April and June was 1.9% of the UK's gross domestic product (GDP), representing the 10th highest period since comparable records began in 1993.
new_text:
The White House is "deeply concerned" about Ed Miliband's appointment as foreign secretary, with a warning that Donald Trump has not forgotten scathing remarks the former Labour leader has made about him in the past.
New prime minister Andy Burnham has been cautioned that Mr Miliband has some "serious diplomatic repair to undertake" in the US, after his promotion to the role on Monday sparked anger from the Trump administration.
The former energy secretary has previously clashed with Mr Trumpover net zero and his attitudes to democracy and in 2016 he described the president as "a racist, misogynistic, self-confessed groper".
One source close to the president told The Independent: "There's genuine anger about this appointment."
The two men have been attacking one another for more than a decade. Mr Miliband previously said of the president: "We must do our best at home and abroad to resist the worst of Trumpism... These are dark days for America and the world. There can be little solace right now."
In 2017, he said Mr Trump had "lowered the bar for idiocy" and later joined a Stop Trump March in London. He also criticised the president's "incredibly peculiar relationship with Russia" and accused him of lowering standards in politics.
The new foreign secretary has also described Mr Trump's criticism on social media of the UK's stance on drilling for oil and gas as "irrelevant", with the president highly critical of his "net zero zealotry" and accusing him of blocking the UK from expanding oil and gas operations in the North Sea.
On Mr Miliband's past remarks, one ally close to the president told The Independent: "Trump doesn't forget. He will not forget. Miliband has some serious diplomatic repair to undertake."
A consultant who works with both the White House and State Department added: "It [Mr Miliband's appointment] is going down like a lead balloon. The US government no longer takes the UK seriously.
"Ed Miliband is known in Washington for all the wrong reasons. Ed Miliband is seen as an unreasonable 'nut zero' extremist who blocked North Sea oil production but was happy for the UK to purchase Norway's fossil fuels from the North Sea."
The White House is said to be "deeply concerned" about the appointment even though Mr Miliband has previously described how much he loves the US.
They also noted that Mr Burnham himself said in 2021: "Any UK politician who gave Trump the time of day should be ashamed."
Mr Burnham chose Mr Miliband as foreign secretary partly because he decided against making him chancellor, following a backlash from those on the right of the party.
However, the plan, as revealed over the weekend by The Independent, is for Mr Miliband to reform the Foreign Office around the UK's climate goals and find a roadmap back to spending 0.7 per cent of Gross Domestic Product (GDP) on overseas aid, which was slashed under Sir Keir Starmer.
But there is also anger in the US over Mr Miliband's time as Labour leader when he withdrew his support for bombing Syria after the regime there was responsible for a number of atrocities in 2013. The coalition government lost the vote in parliament as a result and the US was left alone in wanting to take action.
Former prime minister Boris Johnson, who is close to senior Republicans, suggested on Tuesday that this emboldened Vladimir Putin to invade the Crimea in Ukraine in 2014.
He wrote on X: "The failure to stand up to Bashar al-Assad - for which Miliband bears much guilt - led directly to Putin's first invasion of Ukraine. An appalling choice for foreign secretary. The Kremlin will be delighted."
Mr Miliband lived in Boston, Massachusetts, as a boy for brief periods while his father, the Marxist academic Ralph Miliband, was teaching there. He is big fan of baseball and a supporter of the Boston Red Sox. He later returned to the US as an adult in the early 2000s to spend several semesters teaching economics at Harvard University.
Another ally of the president working on his Iranian policy told The Independent that Mr Miliband was not the only choice raising eyebrows in the US.
They said: "Miliband is a bit of an odd choice - as is Wes Streeting as defence secretary."
But former Labour foreign minister Bill Rammell said he believes Mr Miliband and Mr Trump will be able to put their differences behind them.
He pointed out that both Sir Keir and the former foreign secretary David Lammy had developed a good relationship with President Trump despite their previous remarks about him.
Added to that, JD Vance had never been a Trump supporter before becoming his pick for vice-president in 2024.
Mr Rammell told GB News: "I am sure Ed Miliband will be able to build a working relationship."
In a statement following his appointment, Mr Miliband appeared to look towards the EU first, promising to deepen ties with the bloc before speaking about Britain's "essential alliance with the United States".
Mr Miliband pledged to fight for "Britain's interests and our values of democracy, freedom and the rule of law on the world stage".
He added: "I will seek to deepen and strengthen our strategic alliance with the European Union, which is so important for our prosperity and security and at the same time nurture our strong, enduring and essential alliance with the United States."
Steve Bannon, an outspoken ally of President Trump, told The Telegraph: "The Trump administration took the extraordinary step of warning the new prime minister that the United States would view the appointment of "red diaper" baby and self-described socialist Ed Miliband as very injurious to the 'special relationship'. Burnham could not care less - he appointed this twit anyway."
The Independent has contacted Mr Miliband's office but they have declined to comment.
